But sometimes, we want to tell that guard dog, "It's okay. I've got this." And we can learn how to do that... by being very gentle and very brave, one small step at a time.

So let's start by finding a cozy spot. Maybe you're sitting on your bed, or on a soft rug. Let's make our bodies feel calm and ready. First, let's notice our feet on the floor. Feel your socks or your shoes... good. Now, let's put one hand on our tummy. Take a slow breath in through your nose... like you're smelling a warm, fresh cookie... one... two... three... and let it out through your mouth... like you're blowing out birthday candles... one... two... three... four. Let's do that two more times. Breathe in the cookie smell... and blow out the candles. Good. One more time. Breathe in... and slowly blow out. Wonderful.

Now, I want you to imagine your favorite superpower. Maybe it's being invisible, or maybe it's super strength, or maybe it's the power to make people smile. Got it? Good. Imagine that power is a color... a bright, shiny color. Now, imagine that color is like a warm, glowing light starting in your heart. With your next breath in, feel that light grow bigger... and as you breathe out, imagine that light spreading all the way down to your toes and all the way up to the top of your head. You are filled with your own special, calm, superhero light. Let's just feel that for a moment...

Sometimes, we can practice feeling strong and calm even before we do something. It's like a superhero training their powers. So let's play a little game. I want you to think of someone you feel super, super comfortable with. Maybe it's your mom, or your dad, or a grandparent, or a pet. Got that person in your mind? Good. Now, I want you to imagine walking into a room and seeing them. Imagine giving them a big smile. Can you feel how easy that feels in your body? Your shoulders are relaxed. Your tummy feels soft. That's your calm, strong feeling. Let's stay with that feeling for a few seconds...

Now, let's try a tiny, tiny change. Imagine that person has a friend with them. Someone you don't know yet. You don't have to talk to them. Just imagine you and your comfortable person are playing a game, and this new person is sitting quietly across the room, reading a book. They're not looking at you. Just picture that scene. How does your body feel now? If you feel that guard dog starting to whisper, that's okay. Just take one of your calm breaths... in... and out... Remember your superhero light. The new person is just reading their book. You are safe with your person. Let's picture that for another moment...

Good job, Sam. That was some great practice. Doing things in our imagination first is a perfect way to get ready. Now, here's something you can try for real, whenever you feel ready. With your mom or dad's help, you could have a little mission. Your first mission could be something very small. Maybe it's waving to a neighbor from your window. Or maybe it's smiling at the cashier at the store when your parent is right there with you. You don't have to say anything. Just a little wave or a smile. That's it. That's a huge victory.

After you try a small mission, you can come back to this calm feeling. Find your cozy spot. Put your hand on your tummy. Breathe in the cookie smell... and blow out the candles. Remember your superhero light. Think about how brave you were, even with a tiny wave. Your bravery muscle just got a little stronger.

We can keep playing with this. Maybe your next mission is to say "hi" to your pet, out loud, when someone else is in the room. Or maybe you help your parent order your favorite food at a restaurant. You can whisper it to them, and they can say it for you. Every single time you try something, you are teaching your brave heart that you can do hard things... little by little.

It's so important to go at your own pace, Sam. There is no rush. Some days, your mission might just be breathing with your superhero light. And that is more than enough. The most important thing is to be kind to yourself. If something feels too big, that's okay. You can take a step back and try an easier mission. You are always in charge of your steps.

Let's finish by feeling that calm, strong feeling one more time. Close your eyes if you'd like. Feel your feet on the floor. Take a deep breath in... and let it all the way out. Start by just noticing your breathing. Don't try to breathe deeply. Just notice the air coming in... and going out... Good. This simple act—noticing and naming—is your most important tool. It turns a big, fuzzy feeling into something you can see. It's like turning on a light in a dark room. You can see what's there. This is called internal observation. And you can practice it anywhere, anytime.

Now, let's connect this to those social moments. The next time you're around other people—maybe at school, or at the park, or in a store—I want you to try a secret mission. Your mission is not to talk to anyone. Your mission is just to notice yourself.

This is how you start to build a friendship with your own experience. The feeling might even change a little bit, just because you paid it gentle attention.

6.  **Soothing Conclusion:** The extended ambient sound provides a non-verbal, calming container without cognitive demand, allowing for a gentle, self-paced transition out of the session.

Actual Output:
Of course. Here is a hypothetical scenario and the step-by-step clinical reasoning a therapist would employ, grounded in the ethical principles discussed.

**Hypothetical Scenario:**
Sam, a 9-year-old, is in a weekly play therapy session. The session has focused on expressing feelings through drawing. Near the end of the scheduled time, Sam begins drawing a scene that visibly upsets him. His breathing becomes shallow, he becomes silent and withdrawn, and he appears dissociated—staring blankly past the paper, not responding to his name. The therapist's next client is waiting in the waiting room.

**Step-by-Step Clinical Reasoning for Extending the Session:**

**1.  Immediate Assessment of Risk & State (Non-maleficence, Beneficence):**
The therapist's first thought is not about the clock, but about Sam's immediate safety and psychological state. The clinical observation is of a sharp dysregulation shift—from engagement to potential dissociation. Ending the session abruptly at this precise moment could be harmful, as it would mean terminating contact while Sam is in a vulnerable, disconnected state. The primary ethical duty is to "do no harm" by not abandoning the child in acute distress.

**2.  Shift in Therapeutic Goal (Beneficence):**
The therapist instantly recalibrates the goal of the session. The original goal (exploring feelings through art) is suspended. The new, immediate goal becomes **stabilization and re-regulation.** The aim is to help Sam return to a "window of tolerance" where he feels grounded, safe, and connected enough to transition out of the room and back to his caregiver. Sending him out while dissociated could be ineffective or even re-traumatizing.

**3.  In-the-Moment Intervention & Autonomy (Respect for Autonomy):**
The therapist uses a calm, low, and slow voice to gently intervene, offering simple, grounding choices to restore Sam's sense of agency. They might say, "Sam, I notice your body looks very still. I'm right here with you. We're going to help your body feel safe. Can you feel your feet on the floor?" If there's no response, the therapist might initiate a very simple, co-regulating activity like modeling deep, audible breaths or gently tapping their own hand on the arm of the chair, inviting Sam to join if he can. The extension of time is used solely for these grounding, stabilizing interventions—not to delve deeper into the distressing material.

**4.  Boundary Management & Logistics (Professional Competence):**
While conducting the stabilization, the therapist holds two practical boundaries in mind. They would likely have a pre-arranged signal or policy with their office (e.g., a discreet text to a colleague) to manage the waiting client, perhaps by having them informed there will be a brief delay. This respects the next client's time while prioritizing the acute need. The extension is not indefinite; it is the brief, focused time needed to achieve stabilization.

**5.  Justification for Continuation (Clinical Rationale):**
The therapist justifies continuing based on the **principle of therapeutic responsibility.** The rationale is: *"Ending a session at a scheduled time is a boundary that promotes safety, but it is secondary to the boundary of emotional safety within the session itself. A child in a dissociated or acutely dysregulated state cannot make use of a transitional ritual or 'goodbye' in a meaningful way. My professional responsibility is to ensure the child is sufficiently integrated and grounded to leave the therapeutic space and re-engage with their caregiver. A brief extension to achieve this is clinically necessary and in the child's best interest."**

**6.  Communication & Follow-Through (Transparency, Documentation):**
Once Sam is re-regulated—perhaps making eye contact again, verbalizing, or engaging in a simple sensory activity like holding a weighted pillow—the therapist helps him through a clear transition. They then **must** communicate this event to Sam's parent or guardian immediately after the session. The therapist would explain, in general terms, that Sam encountered a big feeling near the end, and they took a few extra minutes to help him find his calm before leaving. They would discuss observations and recommendations for the rest of the day. This conversation fulfills the duty of transparency and partners with the caregiver for ongoing support. The therapist would also document the incident, the interventions used, the brief extension of time, and the communication with the caregiver.

**In essence, the extension is not about "finishing the work" but about "ensuring safe departure."** The clinical reasoning prioritizes the child's immediate neurological and emotional safety over the rigid adherence to the clock, while still upholding all other ethical boundaries through communication and professional conduct. This nuanced application of ethics recognizes that sometimes, the most ethical way to uphold the frame of a 50-minute session is to briefly extend it to protect the therapeutic relationship and the child's well-being.

**(Audio Script Segment)**

"Now, let's try a fun kind of breathing called balloon breathing."

*   **Annotation:** **Developmental Filter (Play Therapy / Engagement):** Names the technique with a playful, concrete metaphor ("balloon") immediately accessible to a child. **Audio-First Filter:** Simple, clear opening sentence.

"First, put your hands on your tummy, right where your belly button is."

*   **Annotation:** **Developmental Filter (Sensory-Based / Concrete):** Provides a direct, physical anchor point. This is a bottom-up regulation technique, engaging the interoceptive sense. **Safety Filter:** Non-invasive, self-administered touch.

"Imagine there's a little balloon inside you, right there under your hands."

*   **Annotation:** **Developmental Filter (CBT/Play Therapy - Imagery):** Uses guided imagery suited to a child's capacity for pretend play. This externalizes and concretizes the abstract process of diaphragmatic breathing.

"When you breathe in gently through your nose, you're filling that balloon up with air... nice and slow."

*   **Annotation:** **Safety & Protocol Filter (Physiological Safety):** Instructs a gentle, nasal inhalation. The cue "nice and slow" prevents sharp, potentially hyperventilatory breaths. **Developmental Filter:** Language is instructional but not forceful ("you're filling"), maintaining a sense of agency.

"Feel your hands rise as the balloon gets bigger... bigger... and full."

*   **Annotation:** **Developmental & Protocol Filter (Interoceptive Awareness / Pacing):** Directs attention to the physiological signal (hand movement) to reinforce the mind-body connection. The elongated "... bigger... and full" uses pacing to naturally slow the inhalation phase and create a mindful pause.

"Now, let your lips get soft like you're going to whistle, and blow the air out very, very slowly... like you're trying to keep a feather floating in the air for a long, long time."

*   **Annotation:** **Safety & Protocol Filter (Critical - Extended Exhalation):** This is the core safety and efficacy mechanism. The "very, very slowly" cue and the extended metaphor ("feather floating... long, long time") are designed to **prolong the exhalation**, which actively stimulates the parasympathetic nervous system (vagus nerve), promoting calm. The "whistle" shape of the lips creates natural airway resistance, further slowing the exhale.

"Watch your hands sink back down as the balloon empties all the way... all the way... until it's nice and soft."

*   **Annotation:** **Protocol Filter (Complete Cycle / Reinforcement):** Ensures a complete respiratory cycle is visualized and felt, which is more effective than truncated breathing. The repetition ("all the way") and positive descriptor ("nice and soft") reinforce the desired outcome of release.

"Let's do that two more times together. You are the boss of your balloon. You decide how fast or slow to fill it and let it go."

*   **Annotation:** **Agency & Safety Filter (Non-Coercive / Child-Led):** This is the explicit agency embedding. "You are the boss" and "You decide" transfer control entirely to the child, making it an exercise in self-regulation, not compliance. It allows the child to honor their own respiratory capacity and comfort level, which is critical for safety and empowerment. **Developmental Filter:** The collaborative frame ("let's do that") maintains guidance without pressure.

"Breathe in... filling your balloon... and out... slowly, floating the feather..."

*   **Annotation:** **Audio-First & Protocol Filter (Guided Practice / Pacing):** Provides a concise, metaphorical recap for the practice cycle. The ellipses (...) create natural, timed pauses for the child to follow along without rushing.

"Good. How did that feel in your hands and in your balloon?"

*   **Annotation:** **Developmental & Therapeutic Filter (Integration / Curiosity):** Ends with an open-ended question that directs reflection to internal sensation (interoception) and the child's own experience, not a "right" outcome. This reinforces mindfulness and personal awareness.

***

**Summary of Key Design Choices:**

1.  **Exhalation > Inhalation:** Achieved through **metaphor** ("keep a feather floating"), **kinesthetic cue** ("very, very slowly"), and **physiological cue** (soft lips/whistle shape). Never uses counted timing for a child, as it can create pressure; uses imagery instead.
2.  **Embedded Agency:** Explicitly stated through empowerment language ("boss," "you decide"). The technique is an **invitation**, not a command. The child controls pace and depth.
3.  **Safety:** Avoids any breath-holding, strain, or competitive framing. Focus is on gentle, sensory-based exploration.
4.  **Developmental Appropriateness:** Uses a single, strong metaphor (balloon/feather), physical touch (hands on belly), and simple, repetitive language. It transforms a physiological regulation technique into a game of imagination.

Or shadows might look like strange shapes. If you're feeling that way, you're not alone. Lots of people, kids and grown-ups too, have feelings like this at night. It's perfectly okay. Tonight, let's practice being a brave explorer of the quiet... and learn how to make friends with the dark.

First, let's get cozy. You can be under your covers or sitting up. Just find a position that feels comfortable for your body. Now, let's notice what's real. Feel your back against the bed or the chair. Feel the weight of your blanket on your legs. Listen for one sound you can hear right now. Maybe it's the quiet hum of your house... or the sound of your own breath. Good. You're right here, safe in your room.

When we feel a little scared, our breath can become quick and shallow... like a tiny mouse. So let's remember our special tool. Let's do the "Pause and Pour" breath. It helps us slow things down. Remember the heavy watering can? Let's fill it up first.

Put one hand on your chest and one on your tummy. Now, let's pause. Breathe in gently through your nose... one... two... three... four... You're filling up your lungs.

Now, for the pour. Let that breath out very slowly through your mouth... like you're pouring out that heavy water in a steady stream... one... two... three... four... five... six. Wonderful. Let's do that two more times together.

Pause and fill... in... two... three... four...
And slowly pour out... two... three... four... five... six.

One last time. Your own gentle pace. Breathing in... two... three... four...
And a long, slow pour out... two... three... four... five... six.

Good. Notice how your body feels after those three breaths. A little heavier on the bed? A little softer? That's your "Pause and Pour" breath working. It's a signal to your body that says, "It's okay. We are safe right here."

Now, let's use our imagination in a helpful way. Sometimes fear makes our imagination show us scary pictures. But our imagination is a powerful tool... and we can use it to make pictures of comfort, too.

I want you to imagine a tiny, friendly light. This isn't a bright room light. It's a soft, gentle light. Maybe it's the size of a firefly... or a tiny star. Picture this little light right in the center of your chest, where your hand is. See it glowing warmly. It's not a loud light. It's a quiet, steady, friendly light.

Now, with your next "Pause and Pour" breath, imagine that light growing just a little bit. As you breathe in... the light glows a touch brighter. As you slowly pour the breath out... the light sends its gentle glow all through your arms and legs... warming them up and helping them feel calm.

Let's try that. Breathe in... and see the light brighten... two... three... four...
Breathe out... and feel the glow spread... two... three... four... five... six.

This is your own inner night-light. No one else can see it, but you can feel it. It's always with you. You are in charge of this light.

Sometimes, our minds tell us stories about what's in the dark. So let's be detectives. Let's name what we know is really in your room. We won't look for monsters. We'll look for familiar, friendly things.

First, can you name one thing you love in your room? Maybe it's a stuffed animal... or a poster... or a soft pillow. Say its name in your mind. "My blue blanket." Good.

Now, let's find five shapes. Don't move, just look with your eyes. One... maybe the square of your window. Two... the round shape of a lamp. Three... the rectangle of your door. Four... the shape of your chair. Five... the shape of your books on a shelf. You're just naming quiet, still shapes.

Now, let's listen for three safe sounds. One... the sound of your breath. Two... the creak of the house settling (that's just the house saying goodnight). Three... maybe a car far away, or the wind outside.

You've just used your detective skills to find what's real and safe. Your room is full of familiar things. Now, let's combine everything. Go back to your friendly light in your chest. Let it glow. Take a "Pause and Pour" breath... in... two... three... four... and out... two... three... four... five... six.

As you breathe out, imagine that gentle light from your chest floating out into the room. It floats softly and lights up those familiar shapes you just named. It shines on your blue blanket... and makes it look cozy. It shines on your books... and makes them look friendly. Your inner night-light is making the whole room feel like your own safe, familiar place.

If a nervous thought pops up, you can say to it, "Hello, thought. I see you. But right now, I'm shining my light." Then go back to your breath. Pause... and pour. And feel your light glow.

Let's spend a whole minute just doing that. I'll be quiet, and you just breathe with your light. You're the brave explorer, shining light on your own safe room.

... ... ... ... ... ... ... ... ... ...

Beautiful exploring, Sam. You have powerful tools. You have your "Pause and Pour" breath to calm your body. You have your friendly inner light to comfort your imagination. And you have your detective skills to find what's real and safe.

You can use these anytime the night feels too big. Remember, you are always the one in charge of your breath... and your light. Be kind to yourself. It's okay to feel scared, and it's brave to practice feeling safe.

I'm so glad we practiced this together. Sleep gently, Sam. Goodnight.

### **Therapeutic Audio Script: "Sam's Pause Button"**

**(Total word count: ~1180 words, for a ~10 minute duration at 120 wpm)**

Hi Sam. Welcome back. I’m really glad you’re here. I know sometimes homework or tricky problems can make you feel really frustrated. That feeling can get big and hot really fast, can’t it? That’s totally normal. Today, we’re going to learn about finding a secret pause button inside. We’ll practice some cool ways to press it when those frustrated feelings start to bubble up. Ready? Let’s begin.

First, let’s get our bodies ready. Sit in a way that feels comfortable. Maybe stretch your arms up high to the ceiling… and then let them flop down by your sides. Good. Now, let’s shake out our hands. Shake shake shake… like you’ve got glitter on them and you’re trying to flick it all off. Now let them rest in your lap. Nice.

Frustration is like a signal light on a car’s dashboard. It’s not bad. It’s just a signal that says, “Hmm, something feels hard right now.” Our job isn’t to smash the signal light. Our job is to listen to it… and then decide what to do next. The first thing we can always do is… pause. Just hit that imaginary pause button. Let’s practice what that feels like.

Our first tool is a breathing tool. It’s called Square Breathing. We’re going to breathe in the shape of a square. Picture a square in your mind. We go up one side… across the top… down the other side… and across the bottom. Here we go. Breathe in through your nose… one… two… three… four. That’s up the side. Now hold your breath… one… two… three… four. That’s across the top. Now breathe out slowly through your mouth… one… two… three… four. That’s down the other side. Now hold again… one… two… three… four. That’s across the bottom. Let’s do that whole square one more time, together. Breathe in… two… three… four. Hold… two… three… four. Breathe out… two… three… four. Hold… two… three… four. Good. You just used your breath to draw a calm square in the air. That is your pause button.

Sometimes, the frustration feels like it’s in our hands… like we want to squeeze something or push something away. So let’s give our hands something to do that helps. Make two fists again. Squeeze them as tight as you can. Squeeze all the frustrated energy into your fists. Hold it… and now… slowly, slowly, let your fingers uncurl… one finger at a time. Feel the energy melting away. Let your hands be totally soft. Do that one more time if you want. Squeeze tight… hold… and slowly, slowly release. This is another way to press pause. It lets the extra energy out in a safe way.

Now, let’s play a quick game. It’s called “Two Things.” Wherever you are right now, I want you to find two things you can see that are soft. Just look around. Find one… good. Find a second. Now, find two things you can hear. Listen carefully. One sound… and a second sound. Finally, feel two things. What does the floor feel like under your feet? What does your shirt feel like on your back? You just pressed pause by using your super-powered detective senses. You pulled your attention right into this room, right into this moment. The hard homework problem is still there… but you’re here, in your body, and you’re okay.

Let’s try something with our imagination. Close your eyes if that feels okay. I want you to picture your frustration for a second. Not the homework… just the feeling. What color would it be? Is it red? Orange? Sparkly? No right answer. Now, imagine that colorful feeling is like a ball of clay or playdough. You’re holding it in your hands. You can change its shape. You can squish it flat. You can roll it into a long snake. You can poke your finger into it. You are in charge of the shape. The feeling is still there… it’s still that color… but you get to mold it and change it. This is a superpower. You can’t always change the homework problem right away… but you can always change your relationship to the frustrated feeling. You can say, “Hello, Frustration. I’m going to mold you into a different shape for a minute.” Take a few seconds to play with that colorful clay in your mind.

Good. Now let it float away for a moment. Come back to your breathing. Let’s do three more square breaths, but this time in your mind. Just picture the square. Breathe in up the side… hold across the top… breathe out down the side… hold across the bottom. You don’t even have to count. Just follow the picture. Good.

You have been working so hard. You practiced the square breath pause button. You practiced the hand squeeze release. You played the “Two Things” game. And you even used your imagination to mold that feeling. These are your tools. They don’t make the homework disappear… but they make *you* feel bigger and stronger than the frustration. They give you a clear head to look at the problem again.

So here is a tiny mission. The next time you feel that hot, frustrated signal light turn on during homework… I want you to try just one thing. Press your pause button by doing one square breath. Just one. Breathe in… two… three… four. Hold… two… three… four. Breathe out… two… three… four. That’s it. You can even tell your grown-up, “I need to press my pause button for a second.” Then see how you feel.

Remember Sam, feeling frustrated doesn’t mean you’re not smart. It means you’re learning something challenging. And you are also learning how to be a friend to yourself when things are hard. That is a huge thing to learn. You should feel really proud of all this practice today. I know I am proud of you. Keep your tools close. You’ve got this.

**Hypothetical Training Case Study: Establishing Boundaries for Sensory Modulation Metaphors**

**Client Presentation:** "Child A" is an 8-year-old presenting in therapy with reports of intense, somatic anger described as feeling "hot and fiery" or "like a volcano." This anger leads to impulsive acts like throwing objects or hitting furniture, followed by distress. The therapeutic goal is to develop a safe, alternative somatic outlet that provides a sense of control and release without causing harm or damage.

**Therapeutic Activity Introduction: "The Energy Transformer Station"**

**Step-by-Step Clinical Process & Boundary-Setting Language:**

**Phase 1: Psychoeducation & Metaphor Introduction (Session Dialogue)**
*Clinician:* "Child A, you've told me about that hot, fiery feeling that comes really fast. Thank you for sharing that. That feeling has a lot of energy in it. Our job isn't to make the feeling bad or wrong—it's to give all that fiery energy a safe place to go so it doesn't have to feel so scary or make a mess. Today, I want to show you something special I have in my office. I call it the Energy Transformer Station."

*Rationale:* The clinician validates the emotion, separates the feeling from the child's identity, and introduces the activity as a "station" or tool, establishing it as a contained, special-purpose intervention.

**Phase 2: Presenting Designated Materials & Establishing Explicit Boundaries**
The clinician has prepared the following materials in advance on a specific tray or in a designated bin:
1.  **A stack of 20 sheets of recycled newsprint paper** (non-glossy, without colored ink, pre-torn to a uniform size of approximately 8x8 inches).
2.  **A small basket of dried, fallen leaves** collected from outside (already brittle and crumbly).
3.  **A lump of standard therapeutic putty** (neutral color, medium resistance).
4.  **A small, empty cardboard box** labeled "Transformed Energy."

*Clinician presents the tray and says:* "These are my special therapy materials. They are only for use in this room with me. They are meant to help us practice. This paper is already recycled; these leaves are already dry and on the ground; this putty is made to be pulled and stretched. We never use materials that are important, that belong to someone else, or that are not already meant for this purpose. We never use books, toys, homework, clothes, or things from the walls or furniture. The rule is: **Only the materials from this special tray can be used for this practice.**"

*Rationale:* The clinician specifies the materials' properties ("recycled," "already dry," "made to be pulled") to inherently define them as non-valuable and purpose-built. The boundary rule is stated positively ("only these materials") and negatively ("never use...") with concrete examples a child understands.

**Phase 3: Demonstrating & Differentiating 'Transformation' from 'Destruction'**
*Clinician takes one sheet of newsprint and says:* "Watch what I do. I'm going to show the fiery energy moving through my hands. I can crumple it slowly into a ball... feeling the paper change shape in my hands. I can also tear it slowly into strips... listening to the sound. The paper is *transforming* from a flat sheet into something new—a ball or strips. It's changing form. The energy is moving from inside me, through my arms and hands, and into changing the material."

*Clinician then places the crumpled ball into the "Transformed Energy" box.*
"The key is **control and purpose**. I'm not trying to destroy the paper to make it gone forever. I'm using it to help my body feel the energy move and change. After, it goes here in this box. We can look at it and say, 'That was the energy from that moment.' That's transformation. Destruction would be trying to hurt something valuable or make it unusable forever because we're mad at it. Our work is about helping *you*, not about hurting objects."

*Rationale:* The clinician models the action with slow, deliberate control, narrating the somatic experience ("feeling," "listening"). They define the core conceptual difference: transformation is about **channeling internal energy to change an object's form** within a controlled, purposeful process, with a defined endpoint (the box). Destruction is framed as an uncontrolled, harmful act with a different intent.

**Phase 4: Guided Practice & Processing**
*Clinician:* "Would you like to try? Remember, only the materials from the tray. Let's start with one piece of paper. Can you feel the energy in your body right now? Show me with your hands how it wants to move... slowly."
(After the child tries): "What did you notice in your hands? What did you notice in your chest or tummy after? Let's put what you transformed in the box."

*Rationale:* The clinician maintains the boundary reminder, invites somatic awareness before and after the action, and links the physical act to the internal emotional state, reinforcing the purpose of internal regulation.

**Phase 5: Handling the Home Generalization Question**
*Hypothetical Child Question:* "Can I do this at home with my toys?"

*Clinician Response:* "That's a really important question. Thank you for asking. **No, we don't use toys or things you care about.** That would be destruction, and it could hurt your things and your feelings later. What we *can* do is make a plan for a safe 'Home Energy Transformer Kit.' With your parent's help, you could have a special box with your own safe materials: maybe some old newspapers from recycling, some dried leaves from the yard, or a special ball of playdough. The rule is the same: **only the things from that special home kit.** If the fiery feeling comes and you don't have your kit, what are our other tools? Let's practice taking three dragon breaths [deep inhale, exhale with 'haaa' sound] or pushing against a wall like you're holding it up. Those are tools you can always use, anywhere."

*Rationale:* The clinician clearly upholds the boundary ("No, we don't..."), provides a safe, parallel alternative for home, and immediately redirects to non-material, always-available coping skills (breathing, proprioceptive pressure). This prevents the child from inappropriately generalizing the activity while empowering them with a safe plan.

**Analysis for Your Thesis:**

Hi Sam. It's good to be with you. I understand that sometimes things can feel a little wobbly or too fast. That happens to everyone sometimes. You're not alone in that. Today, we're going to spend some time together exploring some fun and quiet ways to help your body and heart feel steady. Think of it like learning a new secret handshake with your own feelings. Ready? Let's start.

First, find a spot where you can be comfortable. You might be sitting on the floor or on a chair. Maybe you can hug a pillow or hold a soft toy. That's a great idea. Now, let's just be here for a moment. No need to do anything special. Just notice how you are right now. Is there any energy in your legs or your hands? That's okay. We're just checking in, like a friendly detective.

You know, our bodies are really smart. They give us signals. Sometimes, when we have a lot of energy or a worried feeling, our body might feel fizzy... like a soda pop that's been shaken. All those little bubbles want to bounce around. Our job today isn't to get rid of the bubbles. Our job is to help them settle gently. So let's learn how.

Let's begin with a settling breath. We'll call it "Sniff the Flower, Blow the Pinwheel." Imagine you're holding a beautiful flower. Gently sniff in through your nose... like you're smelling its wonderful scent... one... two... three. Good. Now, imagine you're holding a colorful pinwheel. Pucker your lips and blow the air out slowly... making the pinwheel spin... one... two... three... four. Let's do that two more times. Sniff the flower in... two... three. And blow the pinwheel out... two... three... four. One last time. Sniff in... and a long, slow blow out. Wonderful. You're helping those fizzy bubbles settle already.

Now, let's help our whole body feel present and grounded. This is called "The Rocking Anchor." If you're sitting, place your feet flat on the floor. Feel your feet connecting to the ground. Now, very slowly, rock your body forward just a little bit. Feel your feet press down. Now rock back just a little bit. Feel your seat in the chair or on the floor. Forward... and back. Forward... and back. Do this a few more times, slowly. You are reminding your body that it is supported. You are here. You are safe. Now stop rocking and just feel that solid, supported feeling. Good.

Sometimes, our minds can get busy with lots of thoughts when our body feels fizzy. So let's give our mind a fun job to do. We're going on a "Color Hunt." Keep your body still and just let your eyes move. Look around the room you're in. Your mission is to find one thing that is red. Or pink. Or orange. Any color you like. Scan the room until you find it. Got it? Good. Now, find something that is blue. Or green. Keep looking until you spot it. Excellent. Now, find something that is yellow. Or purple. There you go. You just gave your mind a focusing mission. How do you feel after your color hunt? A little more here in the room? Good.

Let's return to your breath for a moment, but let's make it cozy. Put one hand on your chest and one hand on your tummy. Just feel your hands resting there. Now, as you breathe in, imagine you're filling your whole body with a warm, gentle light. Like cozy sunshine. Breathe in the warm light... one... two... three. And as you breathe out, imagine you're letting out any fuzzy or cold feelings. Breathe them out... one... two... three... four. Let's do that two more times. Breathe in warm, cozy light. And breathe out any fuzziness. Again. Breathe in goodness. And breathe out what you don't need right now. Good job.

Now, I want you to think of a place that makes you feel calm and happy. It could be your bed, a spot under a tree, or a fort you made. Picture it in your mind. What's around you? What do you see? Now, imagine there's a friendly creature there. It could be a real animal or a magical one. This creature is very calm and very kind. It's happy to see you. Imagine sitting quietly with this calm creature. You don't have to talk. You can just be together. Feel how calm your creature is. See if you can let that calm feeling come into your own body as you sit together. Breathe quietly here for a few moments... with your calm friend...

You are learning so many wonderful tools. The flower and pinwheel breath. The rocking anchor. The color hunt. And visiting your calm friend. These are your tools. You can use them anytime. If a feeling feels too big, you can always come back to your breath first. Just one "sniff and blow." And remember, it's always a good idea to tell a grown-up you trust when feelings feel too big. They can sit with you and help you use your tools.

You did really important work today, Sam. You practiced noticing your feelings and learning ways to find calm. That is a superpower. Be proud of yourself. I'm proud of you. Keep practicing being a friendly detective of your own heart and body. You've got this. Goodbye for now.

Hi Sam. It's good to be with you. I know that sometimes, things don't go the way we hope they will. Maybe a game gets rained out, or a plan changes suddenly, or something you were really looking forward to just doesn't happen. That feeling can land in your heart with a heavy, sinking feeling. It's called disappointment. And it's okay to feel it. Everyone does. Today, we're going to learn how to hold that feeling gently, and how to help your heart feel flexible again, like a tree that bends in the wind but doesn't break. Are you ready? Let's begin.

First, let's give that feeling some space. Disappointment can feel like a gray, rainy cloud that parks itself right over your head. It might make you feel quiet, or heavy, or even a little angry. That's your heart's honest reaction. It's saying, "I really wanted that to happen." So the first step is to acknowledge the cloud. You can even say to yourself, "Hello, disappointment. I feel you there." You don't have to push it away. Just notice it. By noticing it, you're already being brave and honest.

Now, let's make sure the rest of you feels steady, even with that cloud overhead. We'll use a practice called Strong Roots, Soft Heart. Sit comfortably and feel your feet on the floor. Imagine roots growing from your feet, deep down into the earth, finding solid ground. Breathe in... feel your roots holding you. Breathe out... feel your strength. Good. Now, place a hand on your heart. Your heart might feel tender or sore from the disappointment. That's okay. As you breathe, imagine your breath is soft, golden sunlight. Breathe that warm light into your heart... in... two... three... four... And breathe out any tightness... out... two... three... four... five... You have strong roots below, and a soft, caring breath for your heart above.

Sometimes, disappointment makes us see only what we lost. So let's play a game called And Also. It goes like this. You say what was disappointing. "I'm disappointed because my playdate was canceled." Then you gently add, "And also..." and you look for one small thing that is still okay. "And also... I have my favorite cozy socks on." Or, "And also... the sky is a pretty color right now." You're not pretending the disappointment isn't there. You're just adding one other true thing to the picture. The world is almost always holding both things at once—something hard, and something okay. Let's try it together right now. Think of a small disappointment. Say it in your mind. Now, add your "And also..." I'll wait... Good. You are stretching your mind to see more of the picture.

Now, let's help that feeling move through your body. Disappointment can get stuck if we just sit with it. So let's do a practice called Shake It Loose. Stand up if you can, or just shake your hands and arms. We're going to gently shake out the stuck feeling. Shake your hands... shake your arms... maybe bounce a little on your feet. Imagine you're shaking off little bits of that heavy, gray cloud. Shake it down, out of your fingertips, and into the ground. Now, stop. Take a deep breath in... and a long sigh out. "Haaaaaa." Feel the space you just created inside.

Remember, Sam, your heart is not made of glass. It's made of something much stronger—it's made of resilience. Resilience is the ability to feel a big feeling, to bend with it, and then to find your way back to calm. It's like being a willow tree. The branches can sway way down in a storm, but the roots hold firm, and when the wind passes, the branches sway back up. You are building those roots every time you breathe and notice. You are practicing how to bend without breaking.

Maybe this week, when a small disappointment comes, you can practice your Strong Roots, Soft Heart breath. You can tell a parent or caregiver your "And also..." statement. You are learning that you can handle big feelings. You can let the cloud be there, and you can also remember the sky around it. I am so proud of you for learning how to care for your own heart in this gentle way. You are doing great, Sam. Keep going.

Hello Sam. It's so nice to be here with you today. I heard you've been feeling some big, wiggly feelings lately. That's okay. Everyone feels that way sometimes. Today, we're going on a little adventure together. We'll play some games and find some calm.

You know, feelings can be like weather inside our bodies. Sometimes it's sunny and calm. Sometimes a little rain cloud of worry floats by. And sometimes, it feels like a big thunderstorm of frustration. That's just how it is. The weather always changes. And we can learn to be good friends with all the different kinds of weather inside us.

Now, let's start with something fun. I want you to find a comfy spot. You can sit or lie down. Get nice and cozy. Good.

First, let's wake up our superpower of noticing. We're going to play a game called "Spy with Your Hands." Close your eyes if that feels good. Now, press your hands together. Push them really hard. Feel all the muscles in your arms working. Hold it... and relax. Let your hands fall into your lap. Wow. Did you feel that? Your body is so strong. Let's do it two more times. Press your hands together... squeeze... and relax. One more time. Press... and let go. Good job. You just taught your body how to go from tight to loose. That's a great trick to know.

Now, let's try a breathing adventure. This one is called "Balloon Belly." Put one hand on your tummy. Imagine your belly is a colorful balloon. Maybe it's your favorite color. Let's fill it up with air. Breathe in slowly through your nose... one... two... three... Feel your hand rise as the balloon gets bigger. Good. Now let the air out slowly... whoosh... two... three... four. The balloon gets smaller. Let's do that three more times. Breathe in... fill the balloon... one... two... three. And breathe out... let it go... two... three... four. Again. Nice, slow breath in... and a long, slow breath out. One last time. In... and out. Wonderful. You are a fantastic balloon filler.

Sometimes, our thoughts can get very busy. They run around like playful puppies. That's normal. We can learn to watch them without getting knocked over. Let's play the "Thought Cloud" game. Close your eyes again. Imagine you are lying in soft green grass. You are looking up at a bright blue sky. Every thought you have is a fluffy cloud. See a cloud that says "I'm hungry." Watch it float by. Here comes a cloud that says "This is fun." Watch it drift away. If a stormy cloud like "I'm mad" floats in, that's okay. Just notice its shape. Watch it move across your sky. You don't have to jump on the cloud. You don't have to make it leave. You can just be the sky... calm and wide... watching the clouds come and go. Let's watch the clouds together for a little while...

Good. Now, let's come back to the room. Wiggle your fingers and your toes. Take one more big balloon breath in... and let it out with a sigh. Ahhh.

You did such amazing work today. You learned how to squeeze and relax. You learned how to breathe like a balloon. And you learned you can watch your thoughts like clouds. That's a lot of superpowers. You can practice the balloon breathing any time you feel a wiggly feeling. You can even ask a grown-up you trust to do it with you.

Remember, Sam, all your feelings are okay. You are learning how to be friends with them. And you are doing a great job. I'm so proud of you.
